> to cause a computer program to resume execution after a failure, using status and results recorded at a checkpoint

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## restart point

> point in a computer program at which execution can be restarted following a failure (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## result

> information returned to the client

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765l:2024_

---

## RET

> record element type (ISO/IEC 20926:2009 Software and systems engineering -- Software measurement -- IFPUG functional size measurement method 2009, 4)

_Source: ISO/IEC 20968:2002 Software engineering -- Mk II Function Point Analysis -- Counting Practices Manual, 4_

---

## retainage

> portion of a contract payment that is withheld until contract completion to help ensure full performance of the contract terms

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## retesting

> testing performed to check that modifications made to correct a fault have successfully removed the fault (ISO/IEC/IEEE 29119-2:2021, Software and systems engineering--Software testing--Part 2: Test processes, 3.15)

---

## retirement

> withdrawal of active support by the operation and maintenance organization, partial or total replacement by a new system, or installation of an upgraded system

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 12207:2026 Systems and software engineering--Software life cycle processes, 3.1.46_

---

## retirement phase

> period of time in the software life cycle during which support for a software product is terminated (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## retrospective

> regularly occurring workshop in which participants explore their work and results in order to improve both the process and product (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## retrospective meeting

> a team meeting at the end of an iterative cycle or at the end of a software project to reflect on what went well, what was learned, and what should be done differently next time (Software Extension to the PMBOK(R) Guide Fifth Edition)

---

## retrospective trace

> trace produced from historical data recorded during the execution of a computer program (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## return

> to transfer control from a software module to the module that called it

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## return code

> code used to influence the execution of a calling module following a return from a called module

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## return on investment (ROI)

> ratio of revenue from output (product or service) to development and production costs, which determines whether an organization benefits from performing an action to produce something

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## return value

> value assigned to a parameter by a called module for access by the calling module (ISO/IEC/IEEE

---

## reusability

> capability of a product to be used as assets in more than one system, or in building other assets (ISO/IEC 25010:2023, Systems and software engineering--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE)--Product quality model, 3.7.2)

---

## reusable

> pertaining to a software module or other work product that can be used in more than one computer program or software system

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## reusable product

> system, software, or hardware product developed for one use but having other uses, or one developed specifically to be usable on multiple projects or in multiple roles on one project (IEEE 1012-2024 IEEE Standard for System, Software, and Hardware Verification and Validation, 3.1)

---

## reusable software product

> a software product developed for one use but having other uses, or one developed specifically to be usable on multiple projects or in multiple roles on one project. (IEEE 1012-2024 IEEE Standard for System, Software, and Hardware Verification and Validation, 3.1.25)

---

## reuse

> use of an asset in the solution of different problems

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765j:2021_

---

## reused source statement

> unmodified source statement obtained for the product from an external source

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## revealed requirement

> requirement that is derived when a system produces an anomaly or behavior that no explicit requirement prohibits or allows (IEEE 982:2024, Standard for Measures of the Software Aspects of Dependability, 3.1)

---

## revenue alternative

> alternative that is described in terms of its complete cash-flow stream with expense and income (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## reverse engineering

> determining what existing software will do and how it is constructed (to make intelligent changes)

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## reversibility

> degree to which a cloud service provides the process for the customer to retrieve their data and application artifacts and for the provider to delete all customer data as well as contractually specified cloud service- derived data after the agreed-upon period (ISO/IEC TS 25052-1:2022, Systems and software engineering--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE): cloud services--Part 1: Quality model, 3.1.6.2)

---

## reversible execution

> debugging technique in which a history of program execution is recorded and then

---

## review

> process, which can include a meeting, in which work products are presented to some stakeholders for comment or approval

_Source: IEEE 730-2014 IEEE Standard for Software Quality Assurance Processes, 3.2_

---

## review folder

> entity for binding one or more related reviews, including a list of the reviews and information common to the reviews (ISO/IEC 23396:2020, Systems and software engineering Capabilities of review tools, 3.2)

---

## review/audit output

> review or audit artifacts that are expected through conduct of the technical review or audit and that can be considered elements of exit criteria (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-8:2019, Systems and software engineering Life cycle management Part 7: Application of systems engineering on defense programs, 3.1)

---

## revocation

> process of revoking an entitlement or entitlement schema (ISO/IEC 19770-3:2016 Information technology--IT asset management--Part 3: Entitlement schema, 3.1.21)

---

## reward hacking

> activity performed by an agent to maximize its reward function to the detriment of meeting the original objective

_Source: ISO/IEC TR 29119-11:2020, Software and systems engineering--Software testing--Part 11: Guidelines on the testing of AI-based systems, 6.1.65_

---

## rework

> action taken to bring a defective or nonconforming component into compliance with requirements or specifications (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## RFC

> request for change

_Source: IEEE 828-2012 IEEE Standard for Configuration Management in Systems and Software Engineering, 2.2_

---

## RFI

> request for information

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765n:2025_

---

## RFID

> radio frequency identification

_Source: IEEE 7005 2021, IEEE Standard for Transparent Employer Data Governance, 3.2_

---

## RFP

> request for proposal

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765n:2025_

---

## RFQ

> request for quotation

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765n:2025_

---

## RFR

> recovery failure intensity

_Source: IEEE 982:2024, Standard for Measures of the Software Aspects of Dependability, 3.2_

---

## right

> privilege or benefit granted by a software entitlement

_Source: ISO/IEC 19770-3:2016 Information technology--IT asset management--Part 3: Entitlement schema, 3.1.22_

---

## risk

> effect of uncertainty on objectives (ISO/IEC 19770-1:2017 Information technology -- IT asset management -- Part 1: IT asset management systems--Requirements, 3.48) (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge

---

## risk acceptance

> risk response strategy whereby the project team decides to acknowledge the risk and not take any action unless the risk occurs (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## risk analysis

> process of examining identified risk factors for probability of occurrence, potential loss, and potential risk-handling strategies

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## risk appetite

> degree of uncertainty an entity is willing to accept in anticipation of a reward (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## risk audit

> examination and documentation of the effectiveness of risk responses in dealing with identified risks and their root causes, as well as the effectiveness of the risk management process

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765h:2019_

---

## risk avoidance

> course of action that removes a risk factor from further consideration

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## risk breakdown structure (RBS)

> hierarchical representation of potential sources of risks (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## risk category

> a class or type of risk (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765n:2025) (ISO/IEC/IEEE 16085:2021 Systems and software engineering--Life cycle processes--Risk management, 3.8)

---

## risk enhancement

> risk response strategy whereby the project team acts to increase the probability of occurrence or impact of an opportunity (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## risk escalation

> risk response strategy whereby the team acknowledges that a risk is outside its sphere of influence and shifts the ownership of the risk to a higher level of the organization where it is more effectively managed (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## risk exploiting

> risk response strategy whereby the project team acts to ensure that an opportunity occurs (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## risk exposure

> potential loss presented to an individual, project, or organization by a risk

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 16085:2021 Systems and software engineering--Life cycle processes--Risk management, 3.10_

---

## risk factor

> potential problem that would be detrimental to a planned activity, project, or program, characterized by the probability of problem occurrence (0 _ p _ 1) and a potential loss (of life, money, property, reputation, and so on) if the problem occurs (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## risk handling

> course of action taken in response to a risk factor (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## risk identification

> organized, systematic approach to determining the risk factors associated with a planned activity, project, or program

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## risk leverage factor (rlf)

> rlf _ (reb _ rea)/rmc, where reb is risk exposure before risk mitigation, rea is risk exposure after risk mitigation, and rmc is the risk mitigation activity's cost (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## risk management

> organized process for identifying and handling risk factors

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## risk management plan

> component of the project, program or portfolio management plan that describes how risk management activities will be structured and performed (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## risk metric

> objective measure associated with a risk factor to be mitigated

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## risk mitigation

> risk response strategy whereby the project team acts to reduce the probability of occurrence or impact of a risk (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## risk monitoring and control

> tracking identified risks, monitoring residual risks, identifying new risks, executing risk response plans, and evaluating their effectiveness throughout the project life cycle

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## risk profile

> description of any set of risks (ISO/IEC/IEEE 16085:2021 Systems and software engineering--Life cycle processes--Risk management, 3.8)

---

## risk reassessment

> identifying new risks, reassessing current risks, and closing risks that are outdated

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765h:2019_

---

## risk reduction

> reducing the probability or potential impact of a risk factor (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## risk reduction measure

> steps taken to reduce or mitigate risk

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 15026-1:2019 Systems and software engineering--Systems and software assurance--Part 1: Concepts and vocabulary, 3.3.9_

---

## risk register

> repository in which outputs of risk management processes are recorded (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## risk report

> project document that summarizes information on individual project risks and the level of overall project risk (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## risk review

> process of analyzing the status of existing risks and identifying new risks (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## risk sharing

> risk response strategy whereby the project team allocates ownership of an opportunity to a third party who is best able to capture the benefit of that opportunity (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## risk source

> element that, alone or in combination, has the intrinsic potential to give rise to risk (ISO/IEC/IEEE 15026-1:2019 Systems and software engineering--Systems and software assurance--Part 1: Concepts and vocabulary, 3.3.10)

---

## risk threshold

> measure of the level of uncertainty or the level of impact at which a stakeholder may have a specific interest

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 16085:2021 Systems and software engineering--Life cycle processes--Risk management, 3.9_

---

## risk tolerance

> the degree, amount, or volume of risk that an organization or individual will withstand

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 16085:2021 Systems and software engineering--Life cycle processes--Risk management, 3.10_

---

## risk transfer

> transferring responsibility for managing a risk factor to another organization or functional entity better able to mitigate the risk factor

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## risk transference

> risk response strategy whereby the project team shifts the impact of a threat to a third party, together with ownership of the response (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## risk treatment

> process to eliminate risk or reduce it to a tolerable level

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 15026-1:2019 Systems and software engineering--Systems and software assurance--Part 1: Concepts and vocabulary, 3.3.11_

---

## risk treatment measure

> action or means to eliminate hazards or reduce risks (ISO/IEC 25059:2023, Software engineering Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE) Quality model for AI systems, 3.1.4)

---

## risk trigger

> predetermined threshold value of a risk metric that triggers invocation of a contingency plan when the risk metric crosses the threshold

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## risk-adjusted backlog

> backlog that includes product work and actions to address threats and opportunities (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## risk-based testing

> testing in which the management, selection, prioritization, and use of testing activities and resources are consciously based on corresponding types and levels of analyzed risk

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 29119-2:2021, Software and systems engineering--Software testing--Part 2: Test processes, 3.16_

---

## risk-free MARR

> minimum attractive rate of return (MARR) that has not been adjusted to address the risk in an alternative, because the risk is considered insignificant in the decision analysis

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## RJE

> remote job entry

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## RM-ODP

> Reference Model of Open Distributed Processing (ISO/IEC 10746-1:1998 Information technology -- Open Distributed Processing -- Reference model: Overview)

_Source: ISO/IEC 19793:2015 Information technology -- Open Distributed Processing -- Use of UML for ODP system specifications, 4_

---

## RNG

> regular language for XML next generation

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 26531:2023 Systems and software engineering -- Content management for product lifecycle, user and service management information for users, 3.2_

---

## roadmap

> detailed plan to guide progress towards a goal

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 26511:2018 Systems and software engineering--Requirements for managers of information for users of systems, software, and services, 3.1.25_

---

## robot

> programmed actuated mechanism with a degree of autonomy, moving within its environment, to perform intended tasks (ISO/IEC TR 29119-11:2020, Software and systems engineering--Software testing--Part 11: Guidelines on the testing of AI-based systems, 3.1.66)

---

## robotics

> techniques involved in designing, building, and using robots

_Source: ISO/IEC 2382:2015 Information technology - - Vocabulary_

---

## robustness

> degree to which a system or component can function correctly in the presence of invalid inputs or stressful environmental conditions

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## ROC

> receiver operating characteristic

_Source: ISO/IEC TR 29119-13:2022, Software and systems engineering Software testing Part 13: Using the ISO/IEC/IEEE 29119 series in the testing of biometric systems, 3.2_

---

## ROCOF

> rate of occurrence of failures

_Source: IEEE 982:2024, Standard for Measures of the Software Aspects of Dependability, 3.2_

---

## ROI

> return on investment

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## role

> participation of an entity in a relationship

_Source: ISO/IEC 15474-1:2002 Information technology -- CDIF framework -- Part 1: Overview, 4.2_

---

## role name

> name that more specifically names the nature of a related value class or state class

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765n:2025, 3.1.167_

---

## role-based reviewing

> technique where reviewers review a work product from the perspective of different stakeholder roles (ISO/IEC 20246:2017 Software and systems engineering -- Work product reviews, 3.16)

---

## role-playing session

> technique of engagement including the relevant stakeholders in an interactive simulation of the dynamic interactions that are part of the system design

_Source: IEEE 2675-2021, IEEE Standard for DevOps: Building Reliable and Secure Systems Including Application Build, Package, and Deployment, 3.1_

---

## roll in

> to transfer data or computer program segments from auxiliary storage to main storage (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## roll out

> to transfer data or computer program segments from main storage to auxiliary storage for the purpose of freeing main storage for other uses (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## rolling wave planning

> iterative planning technique in which the work to be accomplished in the near term is planned in detail, while the work in the future is planned at a higher level (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## ROM

> read only memory

_Source: ISO/IEC TR 29119-13:2022, Software and systems engineering Software testing Part 13: Using the ISO/IEC/IEEE 29119 series in the testing of biometric systems, 3.2_

---

## root arrow segment

> arrow segment of a junction from which other arrow segments branch or to which other arrow segments join (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765m:2024, 2.1.99)

---

## root cause

> source of a defect such that if it is removed, the defect is decreased or removed

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## root cause analysis

> analytical method used to determine the basic underlying reason that causes a variance or a defect or a risk (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## root compiler

> compiler whose output is a machine independent, intermediate-level representation of a program (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## ROU

> responsible organizational unit

_Source: IEEE 7005 2021, IEEE Standard for Transparent Employer Data Governance, 3.2_

---

## routine

> subprogram that is called by other programs and subprograms

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## RPC

> Remote Procedure Call

_Source: ISO/IEC 10746-1:1998 Information technology -- Open Distributed Processing -- Reference model: Overview_

---

## RPO

> recovery point objective

_Source: IEEE 982:2024, Standard for Measures of the Software Aspects of Dependability, 3.2_

---

## RPP

> request for prototype proposal

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 41062:2024, Software engineering Life cycle processes Software acquisition, 3.2_

---

## RS

> run start

_Source: IEEE 982:2024, Standard for Measures of the Software Aspects of Dependability, 3.2_

---

## RSR

> recovery success rate

_Source: IEEE 828-2012 IEEE Standard for Configuration Management in Systems and Software Engineering, 3.2_

---

## RT

> response time

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-9:2023, Systems and software engineering Life cycle management Part 9: Application of system and software life cycle processes in epidemic prevention and control systems, 3.2_

---

## RTC

> real-time clock

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765d:2015_

---

## RTM

> requirements traceability matrix

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering--Vocabulary_

---

## RTOS

> real-time operating system

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765c:2014_

---

## rule

> constraint on a system specification

_Source: ISO/IEC 10746-2:2009 Information technology -- Open Distributed Processing -- Reference Model: Foundations, 11.2.7_

---

## rule-based language

> nonprocedural language that permits the user to state a set of rules and to express queries or problems that use these rules (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## RUM

> resource utilization measurement

_Source: ISO/IEC 19770-4:2017 Information technology -- IT asset management -- Part 4: Resource utilization measurement, 3.8_

---

## RUM creator

> entity that initially creates a RUM (ISO/IEC 19770-4:2017 Information technology -- IT asset management -- Part 4: Resource utilization measurement, 3.9)

---

## run

> entire activation, execution, and shut-down cycle of a system of interest, however simple or complex its runtime architecture may be

_Source: IEEE 982:2024, Standard for Measures of the Software Aspects of Dependability, 3.1_

---

## run time

> instant at which a computer program begins to execute

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## runtime environment

> specific instance of a configuration of hardware and software, as provisioned for a specific operational environment, on which an installed system of interest runs (IEEE 982:2024, Standard for Measures of the Software Aspects of Dependability, 3.1)

---

## runtime platform

> set of hardware and software components that implement the services utilized by the application software

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765l:2024_

---

## RUR

> Reference User Requirements

_Source: ISO/IEC TR 14143-4:2002 Information technology -- Software measurement -- Functional size measurement -- Part 4: Reference model, 4_

---

## RUSP

> ready-to-use software product (ISO/IEC 25051:2014 Software engineering -- Software product Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE) -- Requirements for quality of Ready to Use Software Product (RUSP) and instructions for testing, 4.1.6)

---

## RWD

> responsive web design

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 23026:2023 Systems and software engineering--Engineering and management of websites for systems, software, and services information, 3.1.20_

---

## S-curve

> graph that displays cumulative costs over a specified period of time (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## SaaS

> software as a service

_Source: IEEE 2675-2021, IEEE Standard for DevOps: Building Reliable and Secure Systems Including Application Build, Package, and Deployment, 3.1_

---

## SACM

> structured assurance case metamodel

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 15026-2:2022, Systems and software engineering Systems and software assurance Part 2: Assurance case, 3.2_

---

## SAD

> software architecture description

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-8:2019, Systems and software engineering Life cycle management Part 7: Application of systems engineering on defense programs, 3.2_

---

## SAF

> sensor availability flag

_Source: IEEE 7005 2021, IEEE Standard for Transparent Employer Data Governance, 3.2_

---

## safe integration

> capability of a product to maintain safety during and after integration with one or more components (ISO/IEC 25010:2023, Systems and software engineering--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE)--Product quality model, 3.9.5)

---

## safety

> expectation that a system does not, under defined conditions, lead to a state in which human life, health, property, or the environment is endangered (ISO/IEC/IEEE 12207:2026 Systems and software engineering--Software life cycle processes, 3.1.48)

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288:2023 Systems and software engineering--System life cycle processes, 3.40_

---

## safety criteria

> limits of acceptable risk associated with a hazard (ISO/IEC TS 33064:2025, Information technology Process assessment Process assessment model for safety processes, 3.3)

---

## safety demonstration

> body of evidence and rationale that shows an item is justified as being safe within allowed limits on risk

_Source: ISO/IEC TS 33064:2025, Information technology Process assessment Process assessment model for safety processes, 3.4_

---

## safety failure

> system behaviors or their absence, such that one or more requirements of the safety reliability class are not met

_Source: IEEE 982:2024, Standard for Measures of the Software Aspects of Dependability, 3.1_

---

## safety integrity requirement

> likelihood of a safety-related system satisfactorily performing the required safety functions under stated conditions

_Source: ISO/IEC TS 33064:2025, Information technology Process assessment Process assessment model for safety processes, 3.5_

---

## safety life cycle

> project or product life cycle in which safety processes are performed

_Source: ISO/IEC TS 33064:2025, Information technology Process assessment Process assessment model for safety processes, 3.6_

---

## safety note

> safety-related information that is collected or grouped in a document or section of a document in a meaningful organizational system to explain safety measures, raise safety awareness, and provide a basis for safety- related training of persons (IEC/IEEE 82079-1:2019 Preparation of information for use (instructions for use) of products: Part 1: Principles and general requirements, 3.33)

---

## safety requirement

> requirement that is needed to help ensure the safety of the product

_Source: ISO/IEC TS 33064:2025, Information technology Process assessment Process assessment model for safety processes, 3.7_

---

## safety sign

> sign giving a general safety message, obtained by a combination of color and geometric shape and which, by the addition of a graphical symbol, gives a particular safety message (IEC/IEEE 82079-1:2019 Preparation of information for use (instructions for use) of products: Part 1: Principles and general requirements, 3.34)

---

## safety validation

> assurance, based on examination and tests, that the safety goals are sufficient and have been achieved

_Source: ISO/IEC 23643:2020, Software and systems engineering--Capabilities of software safety and security verification tools, 3.32_

---

## safety-critical software

> software whose failure or malfunction can result in death or serious injury to people, loss of or severe damage to equipment or property, or damage to the natural environment (ISO/IEC 29110-5-1-2:2025 Systems and software engineering Life cycle profiles for very small entities (VSEs) Part 5-1-2: Software engineering guidelines for the generic Basic profile, 3.27)

---

## safety-critical system

> system whose failure or malfunction can result in one (or more) of the following outcomes: death or serious injury to people, loss or severe damage to equipment or property, or environmental harm (ISO/IEC 23643:2020, Software and systems engineering--Capabilities of software safety and security verification tools, 3.15)

---

## safety-critical system

> process engineering and management;

---

## SAIV

> schedule as independent variable (Software Extension to the PMBOK(R) Guide Fifth Edition)

---

## SAM

> software asset management

_Source: ISO/IEC 19770-3:2016 Information technology--IT asset management--Part 3: Entitlement schema, 3.2_

---

## SAM owner

> individual at a senior organization-wide level who is identified as being responsible for SAM

_Source: ISO/IEC 19770-5:2015 Information technology--IT asset management--Overview and vocabulary, 3.30_

---

## SAM practitioner

> individual involved in the practice or role of managing software assets (ISO/IEC 19770-5:2015 Information technology--IT asset management--Overview and vocabulary, 3.31)

---

## SAM program scope

> clear statement listing of all parts of the organization and types of software, assets, and platforms covered by a SAM program

_Source: ISO/IEC 19770-5:2015 Information technology--IT asset management--Overview and vocabulary, 3.32_

---

## SAM tool

> software used to assist in and automate parts of the process of management of software assets (ISO/IEC 19770-3:2016 Information technology--IT asset management--Part 3: Entitlement schema, 3.1.25)

---

## SAMP

> strategic asset management plan

_Source: ISO/IEC 19770-1:2017 Information technology -- IT asset management -- Part 1: IT asset management systems--Requirements, 3.53_

---

## sample instance diagram

> form of presenting example instances in which instances are shown as separate graphic objects (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765n:2025, 3.1.169)

---

## sample instance table

> form of presenting example instances in which instances are shown as a tabular presentation (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765n:2025, 3.1.170)

---

## SAR

> software requirements and architecture review (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-8:2019, Systems and software engineering Life cycle management Part 7: Application of systems engineering on defense programs, 3.2)

---

## SAS

> sample application strategy

_Source: ISO/IEC 26554:2018 Information technology--Software and systems engineering- Tools and methods for product line testing, 4_

---

## satisfaction

> extent to which the user's physical, cognitive, and emotional responses that result from the use of a system, product or service meet the users needs and expectations (ISO TR 25060:2023 Systems and software engineering--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE)--General Framework for Common Industry Format (CIF) for usability-related information, 3.1.9)

---

## satisficing

> decision technique that discards any alternative with an attribute value outside an acceptable range (ISO/IEC/IEEE 21840:2019 Systems and software engineering--Guidelines for the utilization of ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 in the context of system of systems (SoS), 3.1.6)

---

## SBI

> serial bus interface

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765e:2015_

---

## SBO

> select before operate

_Source: IEEE 7005 2021, IEEE Standard for Transparent Employer Data Governance, 3.2_

---

## SBS

> system breakdown structure

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24641:2023. Systems and Software engineering--Methods and tools for model-based systems and software engineering, 3.2_

---

## scalability

> capability of a product to handle growing or shrinking workloads or to adapt its capacity to handle variability (ISO/IEC 25010:2023, Systems and software engineering--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE)--Product quality model, 3.8.2)

---

## scalar

> value that is atomic (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765n:2025, 3.1.171)

---

## scalar-valued class

> class in which each instance is a single value (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765n:2025, 3.1.173)

---

## scalar-valued property

> a property that maps to a scalar-valued class (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765n:2025, 3.1.174)

---

## scale

> ordered set of values, continuous or discrete, or a set of categories to which the attribute is mapped (ISO/IEC 25000:2014 Systems and software Engineering--Systems and software product Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE) -- Guide to SQuaRE, 4.30) (ISO/IEC/IEEE 15939:2017 Systems and software engineering--Measurement process, 3.34)

---

## scatter diagram

> correlation chart with two axes that plots multiple observations of an independent and a dependent variable, resulting in a regression line that defines the relationship of the variables.

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765h:2019_

---

## scenario

> step-by-step description of a series of events that occur concurrently or sequentially (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765e:2015)

---

## scenario testing

> specification-based test case design technique based on exercising sequences of interactions between the test item and other systems (ISO/IEC/IEEE 29119-4:2021 Software and systems engineering -- Software testing -- Part 4: Test techniques, 3.43)

---

## scenario-based reviewing

> technique where the review is guided by determining the ability of the work product to address specific scenarios

_Source: ISO/IEC 20246:2017 Software and systems engineering -- Work product reviews, 3.17_

---

## SCF

> safety-critical function

_Source: IEEE 7009-2024, Fail-Safe Design of Autonomous and Semi-Autonomous Systems, 3.2_

---

## schedule as independent variable (SAIV)

> a date-certain scheduling method for a project with a specific end date, after which the value of the project declines precipitously or a penalty for non-completion is applied (Software Extension to the PMBOK(R) Guide Fifth Edition)

---

## schedule baseline

> approved version of a schedule model that can be changed using formal change control procedures and is used as the basis for comparison to actual results (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## schedule compression

> method used to shorten the schedule duration without reducing the project scope (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## schedule data

> the collection of information for describing and controlling the schedule (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## schedule development

> process of creating the project schedule by analyzing activity sequences, activity durations, resource requirements, and schedule constraints

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## schedule forecast

> estimate or prediction of conditions and events in the project's future based on information and knowledge available at the time the schedule is calculated (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## schedule management plan

> component of the project management plan that establishes the criteria and the activities for developing, monitoring, and controlling the schedule (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## schedule model

> representation of the plan for executing the project's activities, including durations, dependencies and other planning information, used to produce a project schedule along with other scheduling artifacts (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## schedule performance index (SPI)

> measure of schedule efficiency expressed as the ratio of earned value to planned value (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## schedule variance (SV)

> measure of schedule performance expressed as the difference between the earned value and the planned value (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## scheduled finish date (SF)

> the point in time that work was scheduled to finish on a schedule activity. The scheduled finish date is normally within the range of dates delimited by the early finish date and the late finish date. It may reflect resource leveling of scarce resources. (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## scheduled start date (SS)

> the point in time that work was scheduled to start on a schedule activity. The scheduled start date is normally within the range of dates delimited by the early start date and the late start date. It may reflect resource leveling of scarce resources. (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## scheduler

> computer program, usually part of an operating system, that schedules, initiates, and terminates jobs

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## Schematron

> language for making assertions about the presence or absence of patterns in XML documents

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 26531:2023 Systems and software engineering -- Content management for product lifecycle, user and service management information for users, 3.1.27_

---

## SCI

> software configuration item

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765i:2020_

---

## SCM

> software configuration management

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765i:2020_

---

## SCMP

> software configuration management plan

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765d:2015_

---

## SCMPI

> Software Configuration Management Planned Information

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering--Vocabulary_

---

## SCMS

> Source Control Content Management System (ISO/IEC/IEEE 26531:2023 Systems and software

---

## SCN

> specification change notice

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## scope

> sum of the products, services, and results to be provided as a project (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## scope baseline

> approved version of a scope statement, work breakdown structure (WBS), and its associated WBS dictionary, that can be changed only through formal change control procedures and is used as a basis for comparison (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## scope creep

> uncontrolled expansion to product or project scope without adjustments to time, cost and resources (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## scope management plan

> component of the project or program management plan that describes how the scope will be defined, developed, monitored, controlled and verified (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## scope of the FSM

> the set of functional user requirements to be included in a specific FSM instance (ISO/IEC 14143-1:2007 Information technology--Software measurement--Functional size measurement; Part 1: Definition of concepts, 3.11)

_Source: ISO/IEC 29881:2010 Information technology--Software and systems engineering--FiSMA 1.1 functional size measurement method, A.12_

---

## SCR

> secure collection requirement

_Source: IEEE 7005 2021, IEEE Standard for Transparent Employer Data Governance, 3.2_

---

## screen capture

> representation of what the user will see while using the software (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765a:2011)

---

## scripted testing

> testing performed based on a documented test script (ISO/IEC/IEEE 29119-2:2021, Software and systems engineering--Software testing--Part 2: Test processes, 3.18)

---

## SCRM

> supply chain risk management

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-8:2019, Systems and software engineering Life cycle management Part 7: Application of systems engineering on defense programs, 3.2_

---

## scrum

> iterative project management framework used in agile development, in which a team agrees on development items from a requirement backlog and produces them within a short duration of a few weeks (ISO/IEC 29110-1-2:2024, Systems and software engineering Lifecycle profiles for Very Small Entities (VSEs) Part 1-2: Vocabulary, 3.81)

---

## scrum lead

> person who facilitates the scrum process within a team or project (ISO/IEC 29110-1-2:2024, Systems

---

## scrum meeting

> brief daily project status meeting or other planning meeting in agile development methodologies (ISO/IEC 29110-1-2:2024, Systems and software engineering Lifecycle profiles for Very Small Entities (VSEs) Part 1-2: Vocabulary, 3.83)

---

## scrum report

> report that documents the daily activities of a scrum team, recording any problems or issues to be dealt with

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765h:2019_

---

## scrum team

> members of an agile development team working together under the scrum process, usually led by the scrum leader and project owner

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765h:2019_

---

## SCS

> safety-critical system

_Source: IEEE 7009-2024, Fail-Safe Design of Autonomous and Semi-Autonomous Systems, 3.2_

---

## SCU

> SNAP (software non-functional assessment process) counting unit

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 32430:2025, Software engineering Software non-functional size measurement, 3.1.35_

---

## SDa

> submit data activity

_Source: ISO/IEC 29155-2:2013 Systems and software engineering--Information technology project performance benchmarking framework--Part 2: Requirements for benchmarking, 4_

---

## SDD

> software design description

_Source: IEEE 1012-2024 IEEE Standard for System, Software, and Hardware Verification and Validation_

---

## SDLC

> systems development life cycle

_Source: IEEE 7002:2022, IEEE Standard for Data Privacy Process, 3.2_

---

## SDP

> software development plan

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 16326:2019 Systems and software engineering -- Life cycle processes -- Project management, 3_

---

## SDR

> system design review

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## SDRAM

> synchronous dynamic random access memory

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765c:2014_

---

## SE

> systems engineering

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24641:2023. Systems and Software engineering--Methods and tools for model-based systems and software engineering, 3.2_

---

## search algorithm

> algorithm that systematically visits a subset of all possible states (or structures) until the goal state (or structure) is reached

_Source: ISO/IEC TR 29119-11:2020, Software and systems engineering--Software testing--Part 11: Guidelines on the testing of AI-based systems, 3.1.68_

---

## second normal form

> result of a normalization process that transforms groups of data so that each non-key attribute depends on the key attributes of the group of data and all parts of the key attributes

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765a:2011_

---

## secondary data

> data that has already been collected from various sources, such as governmental census or statistical bureaus, nonprofits, academic institutions and consultancies

_Source: IEEE 7010-2020, IEEE Recommended Practice for Assessing the Impact of Autonomous and Intelligent Systems on Human Well-Being, 2.1_

---

## secondary user

> user who interacts with the product to support the primary users (ISO/IEC 25030:2019 Systems and software engineering--Systems and software quality requirements and evaluation (SQuaRE)--Quality requirements framework, 3.17)

---

## secondary window

> window that contains information that depends on information in another window (the

---

## secure hash algorithm (SHA)

> algorithm that is used to verify data integrity through the creation of a message digest from data input(which may be a message of any length) (ISO/IEC 19770-5:2015 Information technology--IT asset management--Overview and vocabulary, 3.33)

---

## security

> protection against intentional subversion or forced failure

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288:2023 Systems and software engineering--System life cycle processes, 3.41_

---

## security accreditation

> formal declaration by management that an IT system is approved to operate in a particular security mode using a prescribed set of safeguards at an acceptable level of risk

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering--Vocabulary_

---

## security authority

> administrator responsible for the implementation of a security policy

_Source: ISO/IEC 10746-3:2009 Information technology -- Open Distributed Processing -- Reference Model: Architecture, 15.1.2_

---

## security branch

> branch, created at the time of a release, to which only security commits are made

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## security control

> safeguard or countermeasure prescribed for an information system or an organization, designed to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of its information and to meet a set of defined security requirements

---

## security domain

> domain in which the members are obliged to follow a security policy established and administered by a security authority (ISO/IEC 10746-3:2009 Information technology -- Open Distributed Processing -- Reference Model: Architecture, 15.1.3)

---

## security failure

> system behaviors or their absence such that one or more requirements of the security reliability class are not met

_Source: IEEE 982:2024, Standard for Measures of the Software Aspects of Dependability, 3.1_

---

## security interaction policy

> those aspects of the security policies of different security domains that are necessary in order for interactions to take place between those domains

_Source: ISO/IEC 10746-3:2009 Information technology -- Open Distributed Processing -- Reference Model: Architecture, 15.1.4_

---

## security kernel

> small, self-contained collection of key security-related statements that works as a privileged part of an operating system, specifying and enforcing criteria for programs and data to be accessed

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## security level

> combination of a hierarchical security classification and a security category that represents the sensitivity of an object or the security clearance of an individual (ISO/IEC 23643:2020, Software and systems engineering- -Capabilities of software safety and security verification tools, 3.17)

---

## security policy

> rules for need-to-know and access-to-information at each project organization level

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## security responsibility

> degree to which the security responsibilities of both the service customer and the service provider are clearly defined, and security incidents are detected and reported by either party (ISO/IEC TS 25052- 1:2022, Systems and software engineering--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE): cloud services--Part 1: Quality model, 3.1.5.5)

---

## security testing

> test type conducted to evaluate the degree to which a test item, and associated data and information, are protected so that unauthorized persons or systems cannot use, read, or modify them, and authorized persons or systems are not denied access to them

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 29119-2:2021, Software and systems engineering-- Software testing--Part 2: Test processes, 3.19_

---

## SEE

> Software Engineering Environment

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 16326:2019 Systems and software engineering -- Life cycle processes -- Project management, 5_

---

## SEE service

> one or more service operations to support life cycle activities for the SEE (ISO/IEC 15940:2013 Systems and software engineering--Software Engineering Environment Services, 2.8)

---

## SEE service acquirer

> actor that acquires an SEE service

_Source: ISO/IEC 15940:2013 Systems and software engineering--Software Engineering Environment Services, 2.12_

---

## SEE service supplier

> actor that supplies an SEE service

_Source: ISO/IEC 15940:2013 Systems and software engineering--Software Engineering Environment Services, 2.13_

---

## segment

> one of the subsystems or combinations of subsystems that make up an overall system

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## segmented executor

> set of physically distinct artifacts, a physical partition of the executor (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765l:2024)

---

## SEI

> serial expansion interface

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765e:2015_

---

## selective dump

> a dump of designated storage location areas only (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## selective trace

> variable trace that involves only selected variables (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## self-assembly product

> product that is intended to be assembled or erected from separate components without the help of a supervisor or trainer and relying only on step-by-step instructions (IEC/IEEE 82079-1:2019 Preparation of information for use (instructions for use) of products: Part 1: Principles and general requirements, 3.35)

---

## self-contained

> process in which no prior or subsequent processing steps are needed to initiate or complete the functional user requirements

_Source: ISO/IEC 20926:2009 Software and systems engineering -- Software measurement -- IFPUG functional size measurement method 2009, 3.47_

---

## self-descriptiveness

> capability of a product to present appropriate information, where needed by the user, to make its capabilities and use immediately obvious to the user without excessive interactions with a product or other resources (ISO/IEC 25010:2023, Systems and software engineering--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE)--Product quality model, 3.4.8)

---

## self-documented

> pertaining to source code that contains comments explaining its objectives, operation, and other information useful in understanding and maintaining the code

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## self-learning system

> adaptive system that changes its behavior based on learning from the practice of trial and error

_Source: ISO/IEC TR 29119-11:2020, Software and systems engineering--Software testing--Part 11: Guidelines on the testing of AI-based systems, 3.1.69_

---

## self-organizing team

> cross-functional team in which people assume leadership as needed to achieve the teams objectives (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## self-provisioning

> degree to which a service supports on-demand self-service (ISO/IEC TS 25025:2021, Information technology--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE)--Measurement of IT service quality, 3.1.8.4)

---

## self-relative address

> address that is added to the address of the instruction in which it appears to obtain the address of the storage location to be accessed (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering- Vocabulary)

---

## semantic error

> error resulting from a misunderstanding of the relationship of symbols or groups of symbols to their meanings in a given language (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## semantic label

> tag that describes the content rather than its format

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765l:2024_

---

## semantics

> meaning of the syntactic components of a language

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765n:2025, 3.1.175_

---

## semaphore

> shared variable used to synchronize concurrent processes by indicating whether an action has been completed or an event has occurred (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## SEMDM

> software engineering metamodel for development methodologies

_Source: ISO/IEC 24744:2014 Software Engineering--Metamodel for development methodologies, 4.2_

---

## semi-formal verification

> method based on a description given in semi-formal notation

_Source: ISO/IEC 23643:2020, Software and systems engineering--Capabilities of software safety and security verification tools, 3.18_

---

## semiconductor

> substance with conductive properties between those of a conductor and an insulator

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765c:2014_

---

## SEMP

> Systems Engineering Management Plan (ISO/IEC/IEEE 16326:2019 Systems and software engineering -- Life cycle processes -- Project management, 3)

---

## sensitivity analysis

> analysis method to determine which individual project risks or other sources of uncertainty have the most potential impact on project outcomes by correlating variations in project outcomes with variations in elements of a quantitative risk analysis model (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## sentence

> linguistic construct containing one or more terms and predicates (ISO/IEC 10746-2:2009 Information technology -- Open Distributed Processing -- Reference Model: Foundations, 7.2)

---

## sentiment

> value (positive or negative) of affect inferred from the systems data source, such as sentiment from text analysis using natural language processing

_Source: IEEE 7014-2024, IEEE Standard for Ethical Considerations in Emulated Empathy in Autonomous and Intelligent Systems, 3.1_

---

## SEP

> Systems Engineering Plan (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765l:2024)

---

## separate documentation

> documentation that can be used independently of the software (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765g:2018)

---

## sequence diagram

> Unified Modeling Language (UML) diagram that depicts time-sequential ordering of interactions, as in a use case scenario of interactions between an actor and some system elements. Can be used to depict sequential and concurrent data flow or process flow (Software Extension to the PMBOK(R) Guide Fifth Edition)

---

## sequential

> pertaining to the occurrence of two or more events or activities in such a manner that one must finish before the next begins (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## sequential clustering

> task-structuring criterion in which objects that are constrained to execute sequentially are mapped to a task

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## sequential cohesion

> type of cohesion in which the output of one task performed by a software module serves as input to another task performed by the module (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering- Vocabulary)

---

## serial

> pertaining to the sequential transfer, occurrence, or processing of the individual parts of a whole, such as the bits of a character, using the same facilities for successive parts (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## serial bus

> shared channel that transmits data sequentially, bit-by-bit

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765e:2015_

---

## serial bus interface (SBI)

> connection for bidirectional serial data communication

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765e:2015_

---

## serial communication interface (SCI)

> unit that enables the serial exchange of data between a microprocessor and peripherals (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765d:2015)

---

## serial construct

> program construct consisting of a sequence of steps not involving a decision or loop (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## serial expansion interface (SEI)

> connection to serve multiple serial channels

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765e:2015_

---

## serial peripheral interface (SPI)

> synchronous (full duplex) serial communication interface used for two devices in embedded systems

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765d:2015_

---

## servant leadership

> practice of leading the team by focusing on understanding and addressing the needs and development of team members in order to enable the highest possible team performance (A Guide to the Project

---

## server

> hardware system or software program which provides a service to clients

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## server object

> object which performs some service on behalf of a client object

_Source: ISO/IEC 10746-2:2009 Information technology -- Open Distributed Processing -- Reference Model: Foundations, 13.4.6_

---

## server-side

> node, cluster or capsule, which: a) contains, or is potentially capable of containing, a basic engineering object that corresponds to a computational server object and stub, binder and protocol objects in a channel supporting operations involving the server object; or b) contains, or is a potentially capable of containing, a protocol object which (possibly via interactions with other engineering objects) can return a reply identifying another server-side

_Source: ISO/IEC 14752:2000 Information technology -- Open Distributed Processing -- Protocol support for computational interactions, 3.3.10_

---

## service

> performance of activities, work, or duties (ISO/IEC/IEEE 12207:2026 Systems and software engineering-- Software life cycle processes, 3.1.50)

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 15939:2017 Systems and software engineering--Measurement process, 3.36_

---

## service agreement

> documented agreement between the service provider and service customer that governs the covered services (ISO/IEC TS 25025:2021, Information technology--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE)--Measurement of IT service quality, 3.3.9)

---

## service alternative

> alternative that is assumed to provide equivalent service to another alternative over their lives; all the revenue cash flows are ignored to simplify the comparison and only the expense cash flows are shown (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## service catalogue

> documented information about services that an organization provides to its customers (ISO/IEC 29110-1-2:2024, Systems and software engineering Lifecycle profiles for Very Small Entities (VSEs) Part 1-2: Vocabulary, 3.86)

---

## service change request

> formal procedure for submitting a request for an adjustment of a service

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765l:2024_

---

## service compatibility

> degree to which a service can exchange information with a customer's systems or other services and perform its required functions (ISO/IEC TS 25025:2021, Information technology--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE)--Measurement of IT service quality, 3.1.2)

---

## service component

> single unit of a service that when combined with other units delivers a complete service (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765c:2014)

---

## service continuity

> capability to deliver a service without interruption, or with consistent availability as scheduled and agreed

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765c:2014_

---

## service delivery policy

> formal, brief, and high-level statement that embraces an organization's general beliefs, ethics, goals, and objectives of services (ISO/IEC 29110-1-2:2024, Systems and software engineering Lifecycle profiles for Very Small Entities (VSEs) Part 1-2: Vocabulary, 3.88)

---

## service design

> creation of a service solution, typically including the components which create the desired functionality, technology architecture that supports the components, the processes to support and manage the solution, the associated measures (internal performance or customer agreed measures), and the supply chain interfaces (ISO/IEC 29110-1-2:2024, Systems and software engineering Lifecycle profiles for Very Small Entities (VSEs) Part 1-2: Vocabulary, 3.89)

---

## service desk

> customer-facing support group for centralized resolution of incidents, change requests, and complaints concerning a service

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765a:2011_

---

## service export

> interaction with the trading function in which a service offer is advertised, by adding the service offer to an identified set of service offers

_Source: ISO/IEC 10746-3:2009 Information technology -- Open Distributed Processing -- Reference Model: Architecture, 14.5.1.2_

---

## service import

> interaction with the trading function which searches an identified set of service offers to discover interfaces at which a service satisfying a specified type is available

_Source: ISO/IEC 10746-3:2009 Information technology -- Open Distributed Processing -- Reference Model: Architecture, 14.5.1.3_

---

## service level agreement (SLA)

> documented agreement between a service provider and a customer that identifies services and service targets (ISO/IEC 29110-1-2:2024, Systems and software engineering Lifecycle profiles for Very Small Entities (VSEs) Part 1-2: Vocabulary, 3.90)

---

## service level agreement information completeness

> degree to which a service level agreement includes the essential information required to set up the quality level of the service (ISO/IEC TS 25052-1:2022, Systems and

---

## service level agreement satisfaction

> degree to which a service satisfies the service level objectives (SLO) and service qualitative objectives (SQO) specified in the service level agreement (SLA) (ISO/IEC TS 25052-1:2022, Systems and software engineering--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE): cloud services--Part 1: Quality model, 3.1.8.6)

---

## service level objective (SLO)

> commitment a service provider makes for a specific, quantitative characteristic of a service, where the value follows the interval scale or ratio scale (ISO/IEC TS 25052-1:2022, Systems and software engineering--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE): cloud services--Part 1: Quality model, 3.3.8)

---

## service maintainability

> degree of effectiveness and efficiency with which a service can be modified by the intended maintainers (ISO/IEC TS 25052-1:2022, Systems and software engineering--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE): cloud services--Part 1: Quality model, 3.1.6)

---

## service management

> set of capabilities and processes to direct and control the service provider's activities and resources for the design, transition, delivery and improvement of services to fulfill the service requirements

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765c:2014_

---

## service management system (SMS)

> management system to direct and control the provision of one or more services (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765c:2014)

---

## service manager (SM)

> role that directly oversees the delivery of services and provides leadership and direction, has decision-making authority on all activities, and is a direct report or peer to the highest level of the organization (ISO/IEC 29110-1-2:2024, Systems and software engineering Lifecycle profiles for Very Small Entities (VSEs) Part 1-2: Vocabulary, 3.91)

---

## service measurability

> degree to which a service provides metered delivery of services such that usage can be monitored, controlled, reported, and billed (ISO/IEC TS 25052-1:2022, Systems and software engineering--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE): cloud services--Part 1: Quality model, 3.1.8.1)

---

## service offer

> information describing an interface, how to bind to it, and the service that can be invoked using it

_Source: ISO/IEC 10746-3:2009 Information technology -- Open Distributed Processing -- Reference Model: Architecture, 14.5.1.1_

---

## service performance efficiency

> degree to which a service meets performance requirements under stated conditions (ISO/IEC TS 25052-1:2022, Systems and software engineering--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE): cloud services--Part 1: Quality model, 3.1.1)

---

## service primitive

> abstract definition of an interaction of channel objects that causes protocol exchanges between the protocol objects in the channel (ISO/IEC 14752:2000 Information technology -- Open Distributed Processing --

---

## service provider

> organization or part of an organization that manages and delivers a service or services to the customer (ISO/IEC TS 25011:2017 Information technology--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE)--Service quality models, 3.3.7)

---

## service provision system

> system to provide IT service to users, including people, processes, technology, facilities and information (ISO/IEC TS 25011:2017 Information technology--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE)--Service quality models, 3.3.6)

---

## service provisionability

> degree to which a service is provisioned to the customer, as needed, automatically or with minimal interaction with the service provider (ISO/IEC TS 25052-1:2022, Systems and software engineering-- Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE): cloud services--Part 1: Quality model, 3.1.8)

---

## service qualitative objective (SQO)

> commitment a service provider makes for a specific, qualitative characteristic of a service, where the value follows the nominal scale or ordinal scale (ISO/IEC TS 25052-1:2022, Systems and software engineering--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE): cloud services--Part 1: Quality model, 3.3.7)

---

## service quality characteristic

> category of service quality attributes that bears on service quality (ISO/IEC TS 25052-1:2022, Systems and software engineering--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE): cloud services--Part 1: Quality model, 3.2.3)

---

## service reliability

> degree to which a service performs specified functions under specified conditions for a specified period of time (ISO/IEC TS 25052-1:2022, Systems and software engineering--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE): cloud services--Part 1: Quality model, 3.1.4)

---

## service request

> request for information, or for a routine change or procedure with previously evaluated risk (ISO/IEC/IEEE 15289:2019 Systems and software engineering--Content of life-cycle information items (documentation), 5.25)

---

## service requirement

> needs of the customer and the users of the service, including service level requirements, and the needs of the service provider

_Source: ISO/IEC 20000-1:2011 Information technology--Service management--Part 1: Service management system requirements, 3.34_

---

## service security

> degree to which a service protects information and data so that persons or other products or systems have the degree of data access appropriate to their types and levels of authorization (ISO/IEC TS 25052-1:2022, Systems and software engineering--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE): cloud services--Part 1: Quality model, 3.1.5)

---

## service supportiveness

> degree to which a service supports customer activities according to the service support scope specified in the service level agreement (ISO/IEC TS 25052-1:2022, Systems and software engineering--

---

## service usability

> degree to which a service can be used by specified users to achieve specified goals with effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction in a specified context of use (ISO/IEC TS 25052-1:2022, Systems and software engineering--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE): cloud services--Part 1: Quality model, 3.1.3)

---

## set

> collection class with no duplicate members and where order is irrelevant (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765n:2025, 3.1.176)

---

## set-up time

> period of time during which a system or component is being prepared for a specific operation (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## SETA

> systems engineering and technical assistance

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-8:2019, Systems and software engineering Life cycle management Part 7: Application of systems engineering on defense programs, 3.2_

---

## SETR

> systems engineering technical review

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-8:2019, Systems and software engineering Life cycle management Part 7: Application of systems engineering on defense programs, 3.2_

---

## severity

> relative detrimental effect of a failure

_Source: IEEE 982:2024, Standard for Measures of the Software Aspects of Dependability, 3.1_

---

## SF

> start-to-finish

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765n:2025_

---

## SFR

> system functional review

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-7:2019, Systems and software engineering Life cycle management Part 7: Application of systems engineering on defense programs, 3.2_

---

## SGML

> Standard Generalized Markup Language

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 23026:2023 Systems and software engineering-- Engineering and management of websites for systems, software, and services information, 3.2_

---

## SGRAM

> synchronous graphics random access memory

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765c:2014_

---

## SHA

> secure hash algorithm (ISO/IEC 19770-2:2015 (corr 2017), Information technology -- Software asset management -- Part 2: Software identification tag, 3.2)

---

## shadow class

> class presented in a view that is specified in some other view

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765n:2025, 3.1.177_

---

## shared asset

> software and systems engineering lifecycle digital artifacts that compose a part of a delivered member product or support the engineering process to create and maintain a member product (ISO/IEC 26580:2021, Software and systems engineering Methods and tools for the feature-based approach to software and systems product line engineering, 3.17)

---

## shared asset superset

> representation of a shared asset that includes all content needed by any of the member products

_Source: ISO/IEC 26580:2021, Software and systems engineering Methods and tools for the feature-based approach to software and systems product line engineering, 3.18_

---

## shell

> computer program or routine that provides an interface between the user and a computer system or program

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## should-cost estimate

> estimate of the cost of a product or service used to evaluate the reasonableness of a supplier's proposed price

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## shrink small outline package (SSOP)

> thinner rectangular surface mount integrated circuit unit with gull- wing leads on the two long sides

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765c:2014_

---

## sign change coverage

> proportion of neurons activated with both positive and negative activation values divided by the total number of neurons in the neural network (normally expressed as a percentage) for a set of tests

_Source: ISO/IEC TR 29119-11:2020, Software and systems engineering--Software testing--Part 11: Guidelines on the testing of AI-based systems, 3.1.70_

---

## sign-sign coverage

> coverage level achieved if by changing the sign of each neuron it can be shown to individually cause one neuron in the next layer to change sign while all other neurons in the next layer stay the same (i.e. they do not change sign)

_Source: ISO/IEC TR 29119-11:2020, Software and systems engineering--Software testing--Part 11: Guidelines on the testing of AI-based systems, 3.1.71_

---

## signal

> atomic shared action resulting in one-way communication from an initiating object to a responding object

_Source: ISO/IEC 10746-3:2009 Information technology -- Open Distributed Processing -- Reference Model: Architecture, 7.1.1_

---

## signal interface

> interface in which all the interactions are signals

_Source: ISO/IEC 10746-3:2009 Information technology -- Open Distributed Processing -- Reference Model: Architecture, 7.1.6_

---

## signal interface signature

> interface signature for a signal interface (ISO/IEC 10746-3:2009 Information technology -- Open Distributed Processing -- Reference Model: Architecture, 7.1.11)

---

## signature

> statement of what the interface to a responsibility &quot;looks like.&quot;

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765n:2025, 3.1.178_

---

## signpost

> text, symbol, or small graphic that helps the user identify where particular types of information are located or where the information in the current display fits into the information for users as a whole (ISO/IEC/IEEE 26514:2022, Systems and software engineering -- Design and development of information for users, 3.1.45)

---

## SIL

> system integration laboratory

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-8:2019, Systems and software engineering Life cycle management Part 7: Application of systems engineering on defense programs, 3.2_

---

## SILS

> software in the loop simulation

_Source: IEEE 1012-2024 IEEE Standard for System, Software, and Hardware Verification and Validation, 3.2_

---

## SIM

> [mobile telecommunications] subscriber identity module

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765e:2015_

---

## simple buffering

> buffering technique in which a buffer is allocated to a computer program for the duration of the

---

## simplex receptacle

> specialization of a receptacle that only allows a single connection at a given time

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765l:2024_

---

## simplicity

> degree to which a system or component has a design and implementation that is straightforward and easy to understand

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## simulation

> model that behaves or operates like a given system when provided a set of controlled inputs

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## simulator

> device, computer program, or system that behaves or operates like a given system when provided a set of controlled inputs (ISO/IEC TR 29119-11:2020, Software and systems engineering--Software testing--Part 11: Guidelines on the testing of AI-based systems, 3.1.72)

---

## simultaneous

> pertaining to the occurrence of two or more events at the same instant of time (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## simultaneous recursion

> situation in which two software modules call each other

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary_

---

## single boot

> having only one boot mode to start a computer (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765e:2015)

---

## single instance approach

> case where all methods of delivery of the same functionality are counted as one (ISO/IEC/IEEE 32430:2025, Software engineering Software non-functional size measurement, 3.1.33)

---

## single-hit decision table

> decision table where any set of conditions is satisfied by one, and only one, rule

_Source: ISO 5806:1984 Information processing -- Specification of single-hit decision tables, 3.2_

---

## single-level encoding

> microprogramming technique in which different microoperations are encoded as different values in the same field of a microinstruction (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## single-point estimating

> estimating method that involves using data to calculate a single value which reflects a best-guess estimate (A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK(R) Guide) -- Seventh Edition)

---

## single-step operation

> debugging technique in which a single computer instruction, or part of an instruction, is executed in response to an external signal (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## single-valued property

> property with a single-valued mapping (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765n:2025, 3.1.179)

---

## SIP

> system integration plan

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-7:2019, Systems and software engineering Life cycle management Part 7: Application of systems engineering on defense programs, 3.2_

---

## SIS

> safety injection signal

_Source: IEEE 1012-2024 IEEE Standard for System, Software, and Hardware Verification and Validation, 3.2_

---

## site map

> textual or graphical overview of the navigation structure of a website

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 23026:2023 Systems and software engineering--Engineering and management of websites for systems, software, and services information, 3.1.21_

---

## SIU

> system in use (IEEE 7005 2021, IEEE Standard for Transparent Employer Data Governance, 3.2)

---

## sizing

> process of estimating the amount of computer storage or the number of source lines required for a software system or component (ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017 Systems and software engineering-Vocabulary)

---

## skill

> observable competence to perform a learned psychomotor act

_Source: INCOSE Systems Engineering Handbook, 5th ed._

---

## skilled person

> individual with relevant technical education, training, or experience to enable perceiving risks and avoiding hazards occurring during use of a product (IEC/IEEE 82079-1:2019 Preparation of information for use (instructions for use) of products: Part 1: Principles and general requirements, 3.36)

---

## SKOS

> simple knowledge organization system

_Source: ISO/IEC/IEEE 26531:2023 Systems and software engineering -- Content management for product lifecycle, user and service management information for users, 3.2_

---

## sku

> stock keeping unit

_Source: ISO/IEC 19770-5:2015 Information technology--IT asset management--Overview and vocabulary, 3.48_

---

## SLA

> service level agreement (ISO/IEC 29110-1-2:2024, Systems and software engineering Lifecycle profiles for Very Small Entities (VSEs) Part 1-2: Vocabulary, 3.90)

---

## SLA compliance

> degree to which the service complies with or conforms to the SLA requirements (ISO/IEC TS 25011:2017 Information technology--Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE)--Service quality models, 3.3.9)

---

## SLC

> software life cycle
